Requested Officers Declaration:

o I declare that all the information provided within this Border Alert Request is true and

correct to the best of my knowledge. I understand that it is an offence to knowingly give false or misleading information contrary to section 137.1 of the Criminal Code 1995 and is punishable by a term of imprisonment of 12 months.

o I understand that once approved, this alert will remain active for a period of 90, 180 or 360

days (depending on alert type).

o I understand that it is the responsibility of the requesting officer to maintain this alert by

making an application to retain, amend or delete the Border Alert prior to the expiry date of this alert, or within 30 days of an alert match activation.

o I understand that if the Border Alert has expired or is deleted, I will be required to submit a

new Border Alert Request Form.

o I understand that the AFP is not responsible for any actions that are taken as a result of

incorrect details provided as part of a Border Alert request, retention and/or variation.

o I also understand any changes or alterations to the alert must be provided to the AFP

immediately, via phone call or email to the supplied NOSSC contacts.

sections.h:
Border Alert Guidelines / State & Territory Police

Urgent requests

NOSSC manages Border Alerts on behalf of a number of stakeholders and prioritises those requests accordingly and processes all requests as promptly as possible. If your request is urgent because of imminent travel, it is essential that:

• Your email clearly articulates the urgency and

• the requesting officer telephones to advise that an urgent request has been emailed.

Confidential Alerts

In some rare circumstances it may be possible for law enforcement agencies to request that a confidential alert be created. These alerts operate in the same way as any other alert, but they are created directly on the AM System, no record of the alert will be on AFP systems, reducing the likely hood of the subject being made aware the Border Alert in place for them.

A confidential alert should only be raised if the investigation is of a very sensitive nature and where it would be prudent to limit visibility of the alert.

The NOSSC Watchfloor Supervisor can be contacted on the details at the bottom of this guide if you would like to discuss further the option of a confidential alert.

Confirmation of the Alert Request

When the alert request has successfully been created, NOSSC will send confirmation to the requesting officer via email. The confirmation will provide the alert reference number, which must be quoted in any future correspondence, and the expiry date. If confirmation is not received to the alert request within 48 hours, it is suggested that the requesting officer contact the NOSSC.

In the event an alert request is received but doesn’t meet requirements, NOSSC will attempt to contact the requesting officer via telephone to discuss what is required for approval. If the requirements cannot be meet NOSSC will reject the request and provide the reason why the alert request was rejected via email.

Where multiple stakeholders have an interest

The AM system only allows for one active alert on a subject at a time. It often occurs that another stakeholder has an interest in the same subject. For example, police may have an interest on a subject, but an active alert is in place by a trustee for a Bankrupt.

When this occurs, NOSSC may choose to combine these requests into one alert without consulting the stakeholders, the case officer would be advised that another stakeholder has an interest but wouldn’t be advised of who that stakeholder is or the details of their alert. The case officer will only be advised that they are the primary or secondary owner of the alert.

Actions that can be requested on an alert

A list of actions that can be requested along with a brief explanation of what each action involves is provided below. It is possible to select different actions for inwards and outwards travel.

When selecting the actions you want ABF officers to undertake, please keep in mind that some actions are resource intense, it is essential that you only request actions that will assist in your investigation of the subject.

The actions requested must be reviewed after every activation, or whenever the Border Alert is being retained to ensure that they are still appropriate and are not utilising Commonwealth resources needlessly.

ADVISE AUTHORITIES - NOSSC will phone alert contact immediately at any time day or night.

IMMEDIATELY Must be updated where contact changes.

ADVISE AUTHORITIES NOSSC will only notify the contact officer of an activation during business hours or via email.

ATTN TO TRAVEL COMPS Attention to travel companions. If identified, travel companions are typically subjected to the same response actions as the alert subject.

ATTN TO EXCESS CURRENCY Where intelligence holdings suggest the alert subject may carry excess currency or BNI’s across the border without declaring. For ABF to search for excess currency, they must be satisfied that the investigation complies with Customs Act or ASIO Act or Intelligence Services Act. Can only be undertaken with a baggage exam.

ASSESS NEED FOR BODY Assess need for Personal Search (s219L – s219ZJ of the Customs Act).

SEARCH (inwards only) Where it is likely prohibited goods will be carried on or in the person.

Under s219, it is the detention officer who must form the view that there are reasonable grounds for a personal search. Can only be undertaken with a baggage exam.

ASSESS DOCUMENTS Assessment of personal, commercial or travel documents of subject. For ABF to obtain a copy of any documents, they must be satisfied that the investigation complies with Customs Act or ASIO Act or Intelligence Services Act. Can only be undertaken with a baggage exam.

ASSESS ELECTRONIC STORAGE Assess any hard drives, electronic storage devices or SIM/IMEI of DEVICE mobile phone, laptop, iPod etc. For ABF to assess items stored on an electronic storage device for potential download, they must be satisfied that the investigation complies with Customs Act or the ASIO Act or Intelligence Services Act. Can only be undertaken with a baggage exam.

Due to the size of modern devices these can take a long time to assess DETAIN Detain and deliver alert subject to custody of a police officer as soon as practicable after detention. Can only be actioned with an Order, Warrant or Bail conditions.

DO NOT ALERT TO LAW Do not make the alert subject aware of law enforcement interest against ENFORCEMENT INTEREST them. This action will allow the traveller to pass through Smart Gate with the appearance of being undetected. This action cannot be requested with a bag exam or associated action.

sections.h:
Border Alert Guidelines / State & Territory Police

VIDEO SURVEILLANCE – ATTN ABF will take video footage of the subject and note the subject’s mode TO MOD of departure from an airport (inwards action only) This action is resource intensive and should be removed after video has been supplied.

100% BAGGAGE EXAM Conduct 100% exam of alert subject’s baggage. Or this action can also be used to allow for law enforcement response where ABF has no power to detain, for example. Warrants for state and territory offences not prescribed under s219ZJAA of the Customs Act 1901.

Requests to undertake a baggage examination

Where a baggage examination is requested, a legislative reference to the specific offence must be provided on the Border Alert Request Form.

ABF have provided advice concerning the legislative requirements of baggage exams, and the actions of copying documents and examination of electronic devices.

A mobile phone is considered to be a document for the purposes of the Customs Act which defines ‘document’ in section 4 to include ‘any article or material from which sounds, images or writing is capable of being produced with or without the aid of a computer or of some other device’.

ABF officers are only permitted to copy documents and/or download electronic devices if the offence relates to a prescribed Act as outlined in Section 118 of the Customs Act 1901.

The below table outlines the relevant Commonwealth Acts that are prescribed by ABF that fit within section 186A of the Customs Act. This table can be used by members for reference purposes.

sections.h:
Border Alert Guidelines / State & Territory Police

16 Migration Act 1958

17 Proceeds of Crime Act 1987

18 Torres Strait Fisheries Act 1984

S186 of the Customs Act provides the general power to examine goods subject to customs control at a customs place.

Section 186A (1) of the Act gives an officer the power to make a copy or take an extract from a document that has been examined under section 186 if, as a result of that examination, the officer is satisfied that the document may contain information relevant to the following (as per s 186A(1)(b)):

i. an importation or exportation, or to a proposed importation or exportation, of prohibited goods; or ii. the commission or attempted commission of any other offence against this Act or any offence against a prescribed Act; or

iii. the performance of functions under section 17 of the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979; or

iv. the performance of functions under section 6 of the Intelligence Services Act 2001; or v. security (within the meaning of section 4 of the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979).

The definition of an ‘offence’ under s 183UA of the Customs Act includes an offence against the Customs Act, an offence against the Commerce (Trade Descriptions) Act 1905, an offence against s 72.13 or Division 307 or 361 of the Criminal Code 1995, or an offence against a provision in Subdivision 308-A in Schedule 1 to the Taxation Administration Act 1953. This definition excludes an offence against the Firearms Act or any other state and territory legislation.

It is requested that relevant information be provided on the Border Request Form to guide ABF officers in what is required in the baggage examination. For example, if “ASSESS ELECTRONIC STORAGE DEVICE” is selected as an action, an explanation should be provided that specifically details what ABF officers should looking for when assessing the subject’s mobile device (SIM card numbers, contact details, recent calls, stored messages etc.), including the commonwealth legislative reference. Failure to provide a legislative reference may result in ABF not undertaking the requested baggage examination.

Subject to Warrant or on Bail

If the Arrest Warrant refers to Commonwealth offences, ABF officers will detain the alert subject. If the alert subject is on Bail for Commonwealth offences, and the Bail conditions include the condition that the person is not to depart Australia, ABF officers are also able to detain the subject.

A copy of the original Bail Undertaking/Conditions or the Arrest Warrant should be sent with the Border Alert Request Form. The NOSSC will not obtain a copy of any Warrants or Bail documents from any Department or Warrant Bureau (State or Federal) on behalf of police officers for Border Alert requests for subjects of a Warrant or Bail, the police officer will need to have their relevant Department or Bureau forward a copy of the Warrant (a not for execution copy will suffice) or Bail to NOSSC as soon as possible to prevent delay in processing the Alert Request for a subject on a Warrant or Bail. A Border Alert request for a Warrant or Bail cannot be approved without these documents.

The legislative reference to the offences under which the person has been charged, or the warrant has been issued must be quoted in the Offence Details field on the Border Alert Request Form.

It is essential that information stated within Border Alerts remain current and accurate. The NOSSC must be immediately advised of all changes to the status of Warrants or Bail conditions to prevent unnecessary intervention and action by ABF and AFP officers.

sections.h:
Border Alert Guidelines / State & Territory Police

ABF officers will contact the NOSSC who will request AFP officers to attend the customs control point. The case officer will be contacted by a NOSSC member as soon as possible after the Border Alert has triggered to advise that the Border Alert subject has been taken into custody.

Permission to travel

Except for persons subject to travel restrictions included in bail conditions or court orders, there must be a reasonable expectation of travel to and/or from Australia when requesting a Border Alert.

Request for Passenger (PAX) Cards, photos or video surveillance PAX cards

Copies of passenger (PAX) cards for subjects including their travelling companions can no-longer be requested as an alert action on a Border Alert. This is in response to ABF moving away from providing physical PAX cards to the AFP for further dissemination and Processing.

To obtain a copy of a PAX card, the following Request for Information – Law Enforcement Agencies and other Government Agencies form will need to be submitted directly to ABF. This form can be submitted without a Border Alert being in place.

Photos and Video Surveillance

Passenger Photos and Video surveillance can be selected as an Alert action, however obtaining video surveillance requires significant ABF resources and should only be requested if the footage will genuinely assist your investigation.

It is assumed that only one photo and/or one video of a subject is required, and after an alert has matched against the subject an amendment should be submitted to NOSSC to have these actions removed.

Photos and videos are forwarded by ABF to the NOSSC via AFP airport members by internal mail. Currently there are no procedures in place for ABF to obtain these electronically. Therefore, it will be some weeks before these are provided to the case officer.

NOSSC will only email photos to a protected email address, therefore the protected email address should be provided within the request form if a photo is required. If no protected email address is provided, NOSSC will forward the photo via secure safe hands delivery.

Video surveillance is provided to NOSSC as a DVD, and are subject to a protected security classification, these DVD’s can only be provided to the case officer via safe hands delivery.

A postal address that is not a PO Box should be provided within the request form if a photo or video is required.

Notification of travel

When the alert triggers at an airport or seaport ABF officers will advise the NOSSC of the alert activation.

Depending on the notification selected within the alert request, the NOSSC will notify the contact officer either immediately or during business hours advising of the alert activation using the contact details provided.

The inability for the NOSSC to contact the contact officer for an ADVISE AUTHORITIES IMMEDIATELY action, will result in unnecessarily delay, and the subject maybe permitted to, or denied travel. The AFP does not accept any liability for incorrect actions taken because of the contact officer being unavailable. For this reason, it is essential that contact details provided are correct and if immediate notification is required, the contact provided is available 24 hours 7 days a week.

sections.h:
Border Alert Guidelines / State & Territory Police

Maintenance of alerts

Border Alerts will normally remain active for period of 90, 180 or 360 days (depending on the alert type), if it is required to keep the alert active for an extended period, an email will need to be sent to the NOSSC before the expire date indicating your desire to retain the alert for a further 90-, 180- or 360-day period. The alert will cease to remain active if a retention request is not provided before the expire date. A new request form including supporting documentation will need to be submitted if the alert is still required.

When an alert triggers at an airport or seaport, the alert goes into a review period of 30 days, if the alert is required for another 90, 180 or 360 day period, an email requesting the retention of the alert must be received with 30 days of that activation, or you can inform the NOSSC member over phone at the time of initial activation that you want the alert to remain active. If a request to retain the alert either via phone or email is not received within that 30-day period the alert will cease to remain active, regardless of if the expiry date provided by the NOSSC is well into the future e.g. 100 days, and a new request form including supporting documentation will need to be submitted if the alert is still required.

If the alert is no-longer required, please do not allow the alert to expire, an email must be received advising to delete the alert, this is to ensure alerts are not on the system that shouldn’t be, and that the subjects travel plans are not affected.

It is the sole responsibility of the case officer requesting the Border Alert to monitor and maintain that alert.

The NOSSC does not send reminders to renew Alerts after the expiry date or after 30 days following an activation.

The AFP and ABF rely significantly on the accuracy of the information provided within a border alert. If at any time changes to the contact details, information, or response actions are required to be made to the alert, it is imperative that the NOSSC is advised at the earliest opportunity, so the required changes are made to the alert.

Where a request for a retention, amendment or a deletion to an alert is made, the requesting officer should expect confirmation from the NOSSC within 72 hours. If confirmation is not received, the requesting officer must contact the NOSSC to ensure that the request is progressed.

NOSSC Contact information

Any general or procedural enquiries in relation to Border Alerts can be directed to the details below.

Location Address Phone/email

When an alert triggers at an airport or seaport, the alert goes into a review period of 30 days, if the alert is required for another 90, 180 or 360 day period, an email requesting the retention of the alert must be received with 30 days of that activation, or you can inform the NOSSC member over phone at the time of initial activation that you want the alert to remain active. If a request to retain the alert either via phone or email is not received within that 30-day period the alert will cease to remain active, regardless of if the expiry date provided by the NOSSC is well into the future e.g. 100 days, and a new request form including supporting documentation will need to be submitted if the alert is still required. If the alert is no-longer required, please do not allow the alert to expire, an email must be received advising to delete the alert, this is to ensure alerts are not on the system that shouldn’t be, and that the subjects travel plans are not affected. It is the sole responsibility of the case officer requesting the Border Alert to monitor and maintain that alert. The NOSSC does not send reminders to renew Alerts after the expiry date or after 30 days following an activation. The AFP and ABF rely significantly on the accuracy of the information provided within a border alert. If at any time changes to the contact details, information, or response actions are required to be made to the alert, it is imperative that the NOSSC is advised at the earliest opportunity, so the required changes are made to the alert. Where a request for a retention, amendment or a deletion to an alert is made, the requesting officer should expect confirmation from the NOSSC within 72 hours. If confirmation is not received, the requesting officer must contact the NOSSC to ensure that the request is progressed. Minimum and recommended thresholds for obtaining, using, and disclosing telecommunications information.

Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979

Key Definitions:

Serious Offence: Defined in s.5D – includes offences punishable by ≥ 7 years (e.g. murder, terrorism, child exploitation).

Serious Contravention: Defined in s.5E – includes offences ≥ 3 years Issuing Authority Judge, Magistrate, or ART member appointed under s.6DB.

Prescribed offence ≥ 3 years imprisonment

Secondary Use Must be for a permitted purpose under the Act (e.g. related investigation).

Notes:

All access must be proportionate and justified.

Destruction obligations apply to records no longer required (s.79, s.150).

Direct all questions and queries in relation to these products directly to SOAU.

sections.h:
This procedure provides comprehensive operational guidance to WA Police Force members for the preservation, access, use, retention and destruction of stored communications in accordance with Chapter 3 of the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 (Cth) (“the Act”).

The Act establishes a general prohibition on accessing stored communications without the knowledge of either party, subject to specific statutory exemptions. This procedure translates those statutory requirements into practical investigative steps to ensure lawful access, proper oversight and compliance with external inspection regimes.

This procedure applies to all WA Police Force investigations where stored communications are sought, whether access is obtained:

1. with a stored communications warrant under s116, or

2. without a warrant where the relevant person has knowledge or has been notified.

This is covered in more detail is Section 5 and 6 or this document.
sections.b: This procedure provides comprehensive operational guidance to WA Police Force members for the preservation, access, use, retention and destruction of stored communications in accordance with Chapter 3 of the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 (Cth) (“the Act”). The Act establishes a general prohibition on accessing stored communications without the knowledge of either party, subject to specific statutory exemptions. This procedure translates those statutory requirements into practical investigative steps to ensure lawful access, proper oversight and compliance with external inspection regimes. This procedure applies to all WA Police Force investigations where stored communications are sought, whether access is obtained: 1. with a stored communications warrant under s116, or 2. without a warrant where the relevant person has knowledge or has been notified. This is covered in more detail is Section 5 and 6 or this document.

sections.h:
Definitions in this procedure adopt the meanings set out in s5 and s6EB of the Act, unless otherwise stated.

Stored Communication

A communication that:

 Has passed over a telecommunications system,

 Is held on equipment operated by, and in the possession of, a telecommunications carrier; and

 Cannot be accessed without the assistance of the carrier (s5 – definition of “stored communication”).

Stored Communications Warrant

A warrant issued under Part 3-3, Division 2 of the Act authorising covert access to stored communications.

Preservation Notice

A notice issued under Part 3-1A, Division 2 requiring a carrier to preserve stored communications to prevent deletion.

Serious Contravention

A contravention meeting the threshold set out in s5E of the Act (generally offences punishable by at least three years imprisonment or equivalent penalty).

sections.h:
The following information is intended to assist an investigator or applicant who is investigating an offence and, as part of that investigation, seeks access to stored communications held by a telecommunications carrier (Telco).

Depending on the circumstances, there are two lawful methods by which access to stored communications may be obtained:

a) The Act permits access to stored communications without the knowledge of the relevant person using a stored communications warrant (s 116 of the Act); or

b) Access to stored communications may be obtained with the knowledge of the relevant person. In these circumstances, a stored communications warrant is not required; however, alternative procedures apply.

sections.h:
To prevent communications held by a Telco from being destroyed before they can be accessed under warrant, certain agencies can give a preservation notice to a Telco requiring them to preserve all stored communications that they hold.

There are two types of domestic preservation notices:

a) Historic — covering the period that commences prior to the carrier receiving the notice and ends at the conclusion of the day on which the carrier receives the notice; and

b) Ongoing — covering the period that commences at the time the carrier receives the notice and ends at the conclusion of the 29th day after the day the carrier receives the notice.

The period for which historic stored communications can be preserved will vary. It is therefore critical that, as part of a criminal investigation, investigators engage with the SOAU Warrant Administration Team as early as possible to coordinate the preservation request.

A Preservation Request form is located on the WA Police Force Intranet site under:

sections.h:
A stored communications warrant authorises access to stored communications which are associated with the person named in the warrant without that person’s knowledge or consent.

Tasking Request Form

An Investigator/applicant is required to submit a Tasking Request Form (TRF) via IDM seeking a Stored Communications Warrant. The TRF must be approved by the Investigator’s/applicant’s supervisor and OIC prior to submission.

If the TRF is approved, the applicant will be contacted by a SOAU Supervisor and provided with an application form to complete.

The completed application is sent to SOAU Covert Applications Team and allocated to an affidavit writer for preparation (for court) to seek a Stored Communications Warrant.

The Investigator/applicant may be required to submit RFI for CCR/Call/Network Data to prove the existence of SMS messages or other stored communications. (The subsequent affidavit must demonstrate reasonable grounds for suspecting that a particular carrier holds stored communications and this data will support that).

Grounds to obtain Warrant

To obtain a stored communications warrant, it is necessary to satisfy the following criteria:

 That there are reasonable grounds for suspecting that a particular telecommunications carrier holds stored communications on its equipment that a person has made, or that another person has made and for which the person is the intended recipient,

 The content of the stored communications would be likely to assist in connection with an investigation by WA Police Force of a serious contravention in which the person is involved, and

PERSON WITH THE PERSON’S KNOWLEDGE OR

CONSENT

The prohibitions contained in the Act do not prevent WA Police Force from having access to stored communications with the consent of, or knowledge of either party to that communication.

It is therefore necessary to inform the person of an intention to access his or her communications. The person could be a suspect, a victim or a witness to an offence.

The process involves serving a Notice of Intention to Access Data on the person and then execute a CIA Search Warrant on the Telco. A Stored Communication Warrant is not required.

The Notice of Intention to Access Stored Communications and a statutory declaration form can be found on the WA Police Force Intranet site:

Blue Book | State Crime | Crime Support and Services | Special Operations and Applications Unit | Warrant Administration | Stored Communications Warrants | (SC) Statutory Declaration.doc

It is important the Investigator/applicant provide SOAU with a copy of the Notice of Intention to Access Stored Communications together with a completed Statutory Declaration to prove service has been affected.

STEP THREE: Search Warrant

Upon receiving a copy of the Notice of Intention to Access Stored Communications and a statutory declaration of service, the telecommunications carrier will isolate and hold the stored communications.

The Investigator/applicant is now required to obtain a search warrant in order to seize the stored communications from the telecommunications carrier.

For a telecommunication carrier based outside of Western Australia, and in order to seize the stored communications, an Investigator/applicant must obtain an Extra Territorial (ET) Search Warrant. Should an ET Search Warrant be required, an Investigator/applicant must contact WA Police Force Proceeds of Crime Squad for assistance.

In certain circumstances, telecommunication carriers which are based in the Eastern States will send the stored communications to their Western Australian offices. Should this occur the Investigator/applicant is required to obtain and execute a Criminal Investigation Act 2006 search warrant on the carrier to seize the stored communications data. An Investigator/applicant should contact SOAU Warrant Administration for current arrangements with each carrier.

10. REVOCATIONS – PRESERVATION NOTICE

Mandatory revocation:

WA Police Force will, in accordance with s107L of the Act, revoke a preservation notice in all instances whereby:

1. The offence under investigation is no longer deemed a serious contravention; or

2. The existence of stored communications during the specified notice period.

a. Will no longer assist with the investigation.

b. Are not related to the person specified in the notice.

Discretionary revocation:

WA Police Force upon having been issued a domestic preservation notice may revoke the notice at any time.

sections.h:
WARRANTS

Under section 122 of the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979, a Stored Communications Warrant must be revoked when it is no longer required, or may be revoked at any time by the Chief Officer or an authorised delegate. (See annexure)

If the reasons for issuing the warrant no longer exist, the Chief Officer or delegate must:

 Promptly inform any other law-enforcement agency’s Chief Officer using the warrant that it will be revoked; and

 Revoke the warrant in writing.

Warrants that have already expired cannot be revoked.

Process

Once it is determined that access to stored communications is no longer required, the Investigator/Applicant must immediately notify SOAU via email.

SOAU will:

 Contact the relevant Telco to arrange revocation; and  Complete the Stored Communications Warrant Revocation template.

 Request Effectiveness Report and Destruction Advice Report from the investigator.

Note: Revocation of an ongoing preservation order must be signed by Inspector in charge of SOAU.

After revocation

In accordance with section 123 of the Act, SOAU will:

 Complete all required statutory notifications; and

 Provide the Telco with a certified copy of the revocation instrument.

Part 3-4 of the Act explains the rules for dealing with accessed information.

Under section 133, it is an offence for a person to do any of the following unless authorised:

 share the information with someone else.

 use the information.

 make a record of the information, or

 give the information as evidence in a legal proceeding, where the information is:

o lawfully accessed information.

o information obtained by unlawfully accessing a stored communication.

o information obtained under a preservation notice; or

o information obtained under a stored communications warrant.

Penalty: Imprisonment for 2 years or 120 penalty units, or both.

sections.h:
Allows a person who receives information under section 139 to further share, use, or record it, but only for the same investigation purpose for which it was originally provided.

Stored Communications Information given in evidence

Stored communications information can be given in evidence only in limited types of proceedings and for no other purpose.

Under section 139(4), this information may be used in:

 A criminal prosecution for a relevant offence.

 Proceedings to confiscate or forfeit property, or to impose a financial penalty linked to that offence.

 Proceedings under the Spam Act 2003.

 Proceedings to take evidence under section 43 of the Extradition Act 1988, where they relate to the offence.

 Extradition proceedings between States or Territories relating to the offence.

 Proceedings to recover a financial penalty for a relevant contravention; or  Police disciplinary proceedings.

Stored communications information must only be used for one or more of these purposes and must not be used for any other reason.

These records must be retained for:

 Three years from creation, or

Records subject to this requirement must not be destroyed before the statutory retention period has been met.

Destruction

Destruction of Section 151 Records

Once the statutory retention requirement has been satisfied, WA Police Force may lawfully destroy records retained under section 151.

The destruction must be:

 Authorised by the Chief Officer or a delegate; and

 Documented to demonstrate compliance and accountability.

Destruction of Stored Communications Information

Information or records obtained by accessing stored communications:

 Are not subject to mandatory retention under section 151, and  Must be destroyed once they are no longer required for a permitted purpose under the Act.

Destruction may only occur where the Chief Officer is satisfied the information is no longer required for any lawful investigative, evidentiary, or regulatory purpose.

Oversight and Accountability

WA Police Force ensures that:

 Retention and destruction decisions are lawful and proportionate.

 Destruction occurs in a way that prevents reconstruction of information; and  Sufficient records are kept demonstrating compliance with legislative requirements and external oversight.

These obligations apply regardless of internal administrative arrangements and support transparency, privacy protection, and independent review by the Commonwealth Ombudsman.

Warrant Codafile (including Electronic Codafile)

A Warrant Codafile is retained at SOAU and will contain:

 Original warrant(s) issued.

 Restricted Record Register(s) [SOAU document].

 Destruction Advice Report(s).

 Destruction certificate(s).

Destruction Destruction of Section 151 Records Once the statutory retention requirement has been satisfied, WA Police Force may lawfully destroy records retained under section 151. The destruction must be:  Authorised by the Chief Officer or a delegate; and  Documented to demonstrate compliance and accountability. Destruction of Stored Communications Information Information or records obtained by accessing stored communications:  Are not subject to mandatory retention under section 151, and  Must be destroyed once they are no longer required for a permitted purpose under the Act. Destruction may only occur where the Chief Officer is satisfied the information is no longer required for any lawful investigative, evidentiary, or regulatory purpose. Oversight and Accountability WA Police Force ensures that:  Retention and destruction decisions are lawful and proportionate.  Destruction occurs in a way that prevents reconstruction of information; and  Sufficient records are kept demonstrating compliance with legislative requirements and external oversight. Destruction of stored communications information and records When stored communications information or records are authorised for destruction, the destruction must be carried out by an authorised officer and witnessed by another officer.

The destruction process must ensure that:

All destruction must be undertaken in accordance with s150 and ensure the information is destroyed.

Any destruction activity completed is to be recorded on the relevant destruction certificate and filed in the relevant Warrant Codafile and then archived and the Warrant Management System updated.

Communications Recording

A record must be made each time stored communications information is shared, whether verbally or in writing, when it is:

 Shared outside the investigation team; or

 Shared with another agency.

These records demonstrate that the use or communication of stored communications information was lawful, necessary, and authorised.

Any sharing of stored communications information, whether within WA Police Force or with an external agency, must be approved by the OIC of SOAU, and recorded on a Restricted Record Register.

SOAU is responsible for preparing the necessary documents for communication to outside agencies.

INSPECTIONS

The Commonwealth Ombudsman perform inspections and provide guidance to the WA Police Force in respect to Chapter 3 - Stored Communications.

SOAU will facilitate inspections and provide assistance, making records available as required for the duration of the inspection.

Inspections by the Ombudsman (Commonwealth)

The inspection will be conducted, and the subsequent report to an agency will be prepared, based on the focus areas listed below. The Ombudsman will place particular emphasis on whether WA Police Force has appropriate policies, procedures and controls in place to ensure that:

 Access to Stored Communications is lawful, authorised and supported by a valid warrant or statutory authority.

 Stored Communications are only accessed, used and communicated for permitted purposes under the Act.

 Records relating to Stored Communications (including applications, warrants, preservation notices, use and communication logs, and destruction records) are complete, accurate and properly maintained.

 Stored Communications and related records are stored securely and protected from unauthorised access, use or disclosure.

 Access to Stored Communications is limited to authorised personnel with a legitimate operational need.

 Stored Communications and records are destroyed in accordance with the Act once retention is no longer permitted or required.

 Internal oversight, governance and audit mechanisms exist to monitor compliance with Chapter 3 of the Act.

 The Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) have been made accessible to all members of staff through the WA Police internal intranet site.

sections.h:
The Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 (Cth) (the Act) specifies circumstances where an Authorising Officer may make an authorisation for the disclosure of telecommunications metadata / prospective data from a telecommunications company to the WA Police.

In October 2015 changes were made to the Act which specified new extra considerations which Authorising Officers now need to make when considering authorising a request for either metadata or prospective data. Section 180F now requires officers to consider the merit of the invasion of privacy in addition to the consideration of the purpose of the information being used under Sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180.
sections.b: The Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 (Cth) (the Act) specifies circumstances where an Authorising Officer may make an authorisation for the disclosure of telecommunications metadata / prospective data from a telecommunications company to the WA Police. In October 2015 changes were made to the Act which specified new extra considerations which Authorising Officers now need to make when considering authorising a request for either metadata or prospective data. Section 180F now requires officers to consider the merit of the invasion of privacy in addition to the consideration of the purpose of the information being used under Sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180.

sections.h:
Authorising Officers are delegated under Section 5AB(1) of the Act. A delegation schedule (attached) provides a list of positions within WA Police with delegated authority pursuant to s5AB(1) provided by the Commissioner of Police. This means that any officer acting into the position specified under the declaration is empowered to authorise disclosures for telecommunications metadata and prospective data under sections 178, 178A, 179 and 180 of the Act.

Although provided legislative authority via the s5AB(1) delegation, Authorised Officers within the WA Police Force must also:

Complete the Telecommunications Interception Compliance course located on the eForce learning platform (must complete every 24 months):

Complete Authorising Officer Declaration and provide to Special Operations Application Unit:

In the case of an officer acting in an Authorising Officer’s position, ensure Higher Duties is submitted on SIMR to reflect the appropriate position:

Authorising Officers must ensure and demonstrate appropriate consideration is given to relevant areas when authorising telecommunications requests under Sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180 of the Act. The specific areas for consideration are provided within this document and should be read and understood by all persons performing an Authorising Officer function.

A recent decision in the Supreme Court of Victoria in the matter of DPP v Osman, [2024] VSCA 210, provides an Authorising Officer with insight into their obligations under the Act.

In the event of a rejection, the Authorising Officer is required to fill in a rejection slip / the reasons field in IDM, detailing the reasons for the rejection providing feedback for the requesting officer. This also allows WA Police Force to show Authorising Officers are complying with their obligations under the Act.

If a request is deemed acceptable and is subsequently authorised, the Authorising Officer must note what time the authorisation was made on the hardcopy certificate (auto recorded in IDM).

For authorisations under section 180 of the act, the Special Operations Applications Unit (SOAU) require all authorised certificates to have a statement from the Authorising Officer stating that they have considered privacy implications and are satisfied it meets the legislative standards required.

For data to be requested to assist with an arrest warrant, there must be an active investigation (IMS I/R), always check the warrant is still outstanding prior to authorisation. If the suspect is arrested prior to authorisation, the request must be revoked.

Extra scrutiny should be applied to bulk data, named variations, address checks and victim related requests, due to the increased risk of privacy concerns.

sections.h:
To assist Authorising Officers when making the required considerations, case officers are required to ensure all available information is provided within data applications. This enables an Authorising Officer to better assess the request as per the requirements of the Act. Case officers are also required to include specific information including Incident Report (IR) numbers, CAD Job numbers, operation name and IDM reference numbers into the application which will assist Authorising Officers in locating the relevant information required to make an accurate determination.

The Case Officer’s application must provide the following information:

The offence being investigated linked to the data requested, The Act and Section for the offence,

The fine or term of imprisonment for the offence,

A date and time range which is relevant/restricted to the offence under investigation and Details provided must match the offence being investigated.

If the original need for the data request no longer exists and no data has been returned, the requesting application must be cancelled. The risk of this occurring increases when data is requested in relation to assisting with actioning an arrest warrant.

When making a request for bulk data, information must be provided to demonstrate the need for the data and what actions have been taken to minimise the amount of data requested.

sections.t: Authorising Officers Obligations under the Act:

sections.h:
Section 178 specifies that an Authorising Officer cannot make an authorisation under this section unless they are satisfied that the information being requested is reasonably necessary for the purpose of enforcing criminal law.

An authorised officer must not approve a request submitted pursuant to s178 for a Cell Tower Data Dump Quote, or court statements requested on a s178(2) form.
sections.b: Section 178 specifies that an Authorising Officer cannot make an authorisation under this section unless they are satisfied that the information being requested is reasonably necessary for the purpose of enforcing criminal law. An authorised officer must not approve a request submitted pursuant to s178 for a Cell Tower Data Dump Quote, or court statements requested on a s178(2) form.

sections.h: Section 178A specifies that an Authorising Officer cannot make an authorisation under this section unless they are satisfied that the information being requested is reasonably necessary for the purpose of locating the missing person.
sections.b: Section 178A specifies that an Authorising Officer cannot make an authorisation under this section unless they are satisfied that the information being requested is reasonably necessary for the purpose of locating the missing person.

sections.h: Section 179 specifies that an Authorising Officer cannot make an authorisation under this section unless they are satisfied that the information being requested is reasonably necessary for the purpose of enforcing an offence carrying a pecuniary penalty or for the purpose of protecting the public revenue.
sections.b: Section 179 specifies that an Authorising Officer cannot make an authorisation under this section unless they are satisfied that the information being requested is reasonably necessary for the purpose of enforcing an offence carrying a pecuniary penalty or for the purpose of protecting the public revenue.

sections.h: Section 180 specifies that an Authorising Officer cannot make an authorisation under this section unless they are satisfied that the information being requested is reasonably necessary for the purpose of the investigation of a ‘serious offence’ or an offence against a Commonwealth / State / Territory law with a maximum penalty of imprisonment of at least 3 years.
sections.b: Section 180 specifies that an Authorising Officer cannot make an authorisation under this section unless they are satisfied that the information being requested is reasonably necessary for the purpose of the investigation of a ‘serious offence’ or an offence against a Commonwealth / State / Territory law with a maximum penalty of imprisonment of at least 3 years.

sections.h:
Before an authorised officer issues a data authorisation, he or she must be satisfied on reasonable grounds that any interference with the privacy of any person(s) is justifiable and proportionate, having regard to certain matters including:

the gravity of any conduct in relation to which the authorisation is sought, including ;

the seriousness of any offence in relation to which the authorisation is sought ;

the seriousness of any pecuniary penalty in relation to which the authorisation is sought;

the seriousness of any protection of the public revenue in relation to which the authorisation is sought ;

whether the authorisation is sought for the purposes of finding a missing person;

the likely relevance and usefulness of the information or documents; and the reason why the disclosure or use concerned is proposed to be authorised .

Gravity, Relevance, Offence, Use, Privacy (GROUP)

These considerations must be explicitly dealt with in the data authorisation request by both the case officer and the Authorising Officer. The amount of detail required will depend on the extent to which an individual’s privacy is being infringed and the seriousness of the offence being investigated.

The Authorising Officer may consider imposing a ping rate as a condition of authorisation for Location-based Services, but otherwise, the Authorising Officer and the Investigating Officer should discuss considerations for variations in the ping rate, in accordance with operational requirements.

In addition to the Investigating Officer recording this action in their Investigation Running Sheet or Investigation Action Register (IAR), a formal email will be required to be sent to SOAU mailbox for record keeping of any adjustment/s in frequency.
sections.b: Before an authorised officer issues a data authorisation, he or she must be satisfied on reasonable grounds that any interference with the privacy of any person(s) is justifiable and proportionate, having regard to certain matters including: the gravity of any conduct in relation to which the authorisation is sought, including ; the seriousness of any offence in relation to which the authorisation is sought ; the seriousness of any pecuniary penalty in relation to which the authorisation is sought; the seriousness of any protection of the public revenue in relation to which the authorisation is sought ; whether the authorisation is sought for the purposes of finding a missing person; the likely relevance and usefulness of the information or documents; and the reason why the disclosure or use concerned is proposed to be authorised . Gravity, Relevance, Offence, Use, Privacy (GROUP) These considerations must be explicitly dealt with in the data authorisation request by both the case officer and the Authorising Officer. The amount of detail required will depend on the extent to which an individual’s privacy is being infringed and the seriousness of the offence being investigated. The Authorising Officer may consider imposing a ping rate as a condition of authorisation for Location-based Services, but otherwise, the Authorising Officer and the Investigating Officer should discuss considerations for variations in the ping rate, in accordance with operational requirements. In addition to the Investigating Officer recording this action in their Investigation Running Sheet or Investigation Action Register (IAR), a formal email will be required to be sent to SOAU mailbox for record keeping of any adjustment/s in frequency.

sections.h:
Verbal Authorisations must not be provided by an Authorising Officer in respect to applications submitted pursuant to Sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180 of the Act.

Although a verbal briefing may be provided to an Authorising Officer, notes and records must be made to demonstrate privacy considerations, relevancy of the data/information sought and the gravity of offence under investigation.

A request may only be submitted to a service provider upon endorsement of the relevant Chapter 4 authorisation (Sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180) document.

Although electronic signatures are permitted under the Act, the WA Police have only limited ability to provide electronic endorsement to applications at this time.

sections.t: Cell Tower and Payphone Data Dump Considerations:
sections.h:
In order to limit privacy intrusion to the maximum extent, Authorising Officers are required to provide additional scrutiny when considering authorisation of Cell Tower Data Dump and Payphone Data Dump requests.

A Cell Tower/Payphone Data Dump request (unlike most other data requests) seeks a significant volume of data from a large number of persons, many or most of whom are not suspected of having committed a serious criminal offence. A Cell Tower/Payphone Data Dump request presents a significant intrusion to the privacy of a large number of persons, for this reason Authorising Officers must ensure the requested parameters are consistent with the aims of the investigation.

In circumstances where an offence under investigation has been identified to have occurred at a specific time/date, an investigator seeking excessive Cell Tower/Payphone Data Dump periods (i.e., 24 hours) should be questioned and must provide detail as to why such a significant time parameter has been requested.

In the event a case officer cannot justify the time parameters requested for a Cell Tower/Payphone Data Dump, the request shall be rejected by the Authorising Officer.

Case officers shall upon receipt of Cell Tower/Payphone Data Dump, be provided advice in regard to the management and quarantine process for data which is identified as of no value or outside of the needs of the investigation.

SOAU will review all Cell Tower/Payphone Data to ensure the data provided by the carrier is within the authorised date/time frame. In some circumstances the carrier may provide data outside of the period of authorisation requiring SOAU to immediately quarantine all unauthorised data, thereby providing the case officer with only data covered by the authorisation.

sections.h:

sections.h: Within the WA Police Force, the following areas are approved to facilitate and provision Telecommunications Data Requests under Sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180 of the Act.

sections.h:
SOAU are the WA Police primary area responsible for the authorisation, provisioning, dissemination and compliance with telecommunications data pursuant to Sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180 of the Act.

SOAU manage the WA Police TI Platform (JSI) and provision all TI warrants for the agency. SOAU are responsible for the compliance associated with all live TI obtained and also facilitate live TI warrants on behalf of the Internal Affairs Unit (IAU).

SOAU also manage the majority of telecommunications data applications (metadata and prospective data). Case officers submit Request for Information (RFI) via the WA Police IDM system which is received by SOAU, processed and forwarded to an Authorising Officer for consideration.

Upon authorisation SOAU prepare the request which is submitted to the service provider. Direct contact with service providers should always be conducted through SOAU.

Once received, the data is vetted and disseminated back to the case officer via IDM.

SOAU maintain all records and provide the central point of contact for all Ombudsman inspections relating to telecommunications data.

Routine Prospective Data (PD) and Location Based Service (LBS) applications are also facilitated by SOAU however authorisation of these applications (routine) will be performed by the case officer’s respective division or portfolio.

sections.h:
SOCC Inspectors are delegated as per s5AB(1) as Authorising Officers and have the ability to consider and authorise applications submitted under sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180 of the Act.

The SOCC will only facilitate authorisation and provision of requests outside of business hours when SOAU staff are unavailable.

sections.h:
Staff attached to IAU perform telecommunications data authorisations pursuant to sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180 of the Act. IAU utilise Authorising Officers from within the Professional Standards Portfolio as per delegation under s5AB(1).

IAU will authorise and facilitate telecommunication data requests (excluding TI warrants and Section 180) and are required to maintain all records and documents associated with such requests and make available for ombudsman inspection as required.

It is accepted IAU may adopt localised practices and processes regarding the dissemination, storage and restriction of access to telecommunications data/information (given the nature of investigations undertaken) however, all processes, considerations and activities undertaken by case officers and Authorising Officers must be in accordance with the WA Police Force Telecommunications Data and Authorising Officers Standard Operating procedures.

The IAU utilise SOAU for all s180 applications and TI warrants however will communicate such requests directly via the OIC of SOAU or Assistant Divisional Officer (ADO), who will ensure appropriate restrictions are placed over the interception platform to ensure integrity and confidentiality is maintained.

The OIC of SOAU and/or ADO will ensure all documentation submitted by IAU for section 180 and TI warrants are secured in a manner as to restrict/prevent access by unauthorised staff.

SOAU will make such records available for ombudsman inspection as required.

sections.h:
SOAU may provide information on the different types of data that can be obtained through a data authorisation. Examples include:

Basic subscriber – customer name, date of birth, billing address, date of connection/disconnection.

Call records – calling party (A), called party (B), time, date, duration and type of call.

Other call record types include:

Cell ID location (identify base station details using a cell ID as the search criteria) Cell dump (identify all calls made through a cell tower. A quote must be obtained from Optus and Vodafone/TPG before an authorisation is made – (Quote not to be requested pursuant to s178).

EBM/VLR/GPS (near real time location of mobile if switched on and roaming).

Complex subscriber – All the information contained in a basic subscriber but the requesting officer must include details of the additional information being sought e.g. copy of contract.

sections.h:
The SOCC provides 24/7 support to frontline WA Police Force Operations and have the capability to facilitate the following telecommunications data functions:

Prospective Data Applications (authorisations and provisioning).

Location Based Services (authorisations and provisioning).

IPND checks

In accordance with the s5AB(1) delegation schedule, the SOCC Inspectors provide 24/7 access to Authorised Officers for frontline officers and case officers.

Case officers may submit telecommunications data authorisation applications for consideration by an Authorised Officer via the SOCC any time a local Authorising Officer is unavailable.

SOCC Detective Sergeants will assist case officers in completing applications or when urgent circumstances exist a verbal briefing may be facilitated in order to expedite the application process.

In any instances where SOCC facilitate the provisioning of a telecommunications data request, all documentation required for compliance is required to be maintained and made available for inspection by the ombudsman when required.

All telecommunications data authorisations processed and facilitated by SOCC must be performed in accordance with the specific guidelines as outlined in this document, especially in relation to:

Authorising Officer delegation.

Authorising Officer considerations.

record keeping and reporting.

Journalist information, and

Ombudsman inspection.

Internal Affairs Unit (IAU)

Specific positions held within the IAU are provided delegated authority pursuant to the s5AB(1) schedule and as such may authorise telecommunications data requests submitted pursuant to Sections 178, 178A, 179 & 180. of the Act.

All telecommunications data authorisations processed and facilitated by IAU must be performed in accordance with the specific guidelines as outlined in this document, especially in relation to:

Case officer obligations.

Authorising Officer delegation.

Authorising Officer considerations.

Record keeping and reporting.

Journalist information, and

Ombudsman inspection.

sections.h:
Each area within the WA Police Force responsible for submitting telecommunications data requests, must ensure upon receipt of any telecommunications data from a service provider, appropriate vetting is conducted of the data received to confirm:

Data/information received is within the parameters of the authorisation date/time.

Any data/information received identified as being outside of the parameters of the authorisation must be immediately quarantined.

The appropriate quarantine register is updated to reflect the details of the quarantined data and why it was quarantined.

Re-submit request to carrier seeking data only within authorised parameters.

Make available quarantined data and register for inspection by Ombudsman as required.

SOAU staff will maintain the Quarantine Register of all data received which is outside of an authorisation.

Should a case officer have used data provided which is identified as being outside of an authorisation, he or she must quarantine such data, contact SOAU and seek legal advice immediately.

sections.h:
SOAU have access to the quarantine repositories located within IDM.

Quarantined – Unused Telecommunications Data

Once investigators have finished vetting the results of cell tower and payphone data dumps and Payphone CCR’s / Reverse CCR’s to limit the intrusion to privacy of uninvolved parties, they must notify the SOAU Intel and Data Team, as per the process hereunder.

Quarantined – Unauthorised Telecommunications Data

To be used by a SOAU vetting officer when data is returned outside of the scope of the original request.

At any time, a staff member, case officer or other identifies or requires telecommunications data to be quarantined, the member will contact the SOAU Intel and Data Team and:

Provide reference number (IDM allocated number).

Advise data to be quarantined.

Advise which folder the data is to be locked down into.

Seek email confirmation from IMU once task completed.

Email SOAU and advise data has been quarantined (for case officers).

Should access be required to this data at a later date a request in writing must be submitted via SOAU for approval.

sections.h:
An Authorising Officer or any Case Officer, must revoke a prospective authorisation as soon as he or she becomes aware the grounds for originally issuing the data authorisation have ceased to exist (section 180(7)).

Upon a decision to revoke a prospective authorisation, the case officer must inform SOAU to facilitate the revocation, inclusive of a formal email.

Once the investigator is satisfied the use of the Prospective Data is no longer required they must request the revocation of the authorisation:

Send a request to revoke the authorisation to SOAU via the section email (Special Operations Application Unit mailbox: Mbx.SOAU@police.wa.gov.au).

Warrant Administration staff will then disable the Operation/Target/Line on the JSI Interception System (WA Police Force Interception Platform).

Warrant Administration staff log into the SOAU “Warrant Management System” and complete a 180 Prospective Revocation for the relevant Authorisation.

Once completed this will generate a Section 180 Revocation document for signing by the Authorising Officer.

Once signed, the revocation is communicated to the telecommunications carrier.

Warrant Administration staff then notify the investigating officer that the authorisation has been revoked.

Should SOAU receive a signed authority for Prospective Data that doesn’t meet the required legislative standard:

Email the investigator “The submitted application requires further attention prior to provisioning with the carrier.”

The email specifies that “should these issues not be rectified; the application will be subject to revocation pursuant to s180(7) of the Act.”

If the matter is not resolved please complete Revocation Certificate pursuant to s180(7) of the TIA Act and have signed by Authorised Officer. Please advise applicant and file application with revocation certificate. (See compliance checklist.)

SOAU Officer: _______________________ Date: __________________________

An authorised application may be subject to formal revocation for the one of the following reasons:

Significant administrative error identified within the application.

The reasons/grounds for the application have ceased to exists or changed to the extent the application is no longer required or requires a new submission.

In each occasion where an authorisation is subject to formal revocation, a copy of the revocation certificate and documentation are to be forwarded to SOAU where the revocation and documents will be retained for inspection as required.

sections.t: Use and Disclosure of prospective information or documents under Section 180 authorisations:
sections.h:
It is an offence to use or disclose any information which would reveal the existence of a telecommunications data authorisation (s180) or to use or disclose a document or information which has been disclosed to a person under a data authorisation. However, there are exceptions to these offences for enforcement of criminal law.

In order to ensure that any use or disclosure of information is done for a permitted purpose, the Act requires the WA Police to keep records of use and disclosure of information in relation to telecommunications data authorisations and telecommunications data obtained under a s180 authorisation.

The records in relation to the disclosure of telecommunications data authorisations or telecommunications data for the purpose of the enforcement of criminal law are kept by the case officer, using the WA Police Use and Communications Log (UCL) template. The information to be recorded in the template includes:

The disclosure or use that took place.

When the disclosure or use took place.

The basis on which the disclosure or use falls within one of the exceptions.

The Act enables the use or disclosure of information or documents if:

the disclosure or use is for the purposes of the authorisation, revocation or notification concerned; or the disclosure or use is reasonably necessary for the enforcement of the criminal law.

for the enforcement of a law imposing a pecuniary penalty; or for the protection of the public revenue.

There are other use and communications responsibilities relating to authorisations obtained under s178, s178A or s179 for existing information or documents (historic information) within s181B and s182 of the Act, which must be recorded as per section 186A of the Act (Secondary Use and Communications).

sections.h:
The Authorising Officer upon reviewing the application submitted must take into consideration several factors and further, must demonstrate consideration of these factors in approving the application. The factors which require specific demonstration of consideration are:

The Authorising Officer holds a position as per s5AB(1) delegation schedule.

That the disclosure is reasonably necessary for the investigation.

Privacy considerations have been satisfied, as per section 180F of the Act.

Current use/possession of the device/number has been satisfied.

If the application relates to Journalist Information, and Reason for approval/rejection of application.

A prospective data application is only authorised once the Authorising Officer endorses the application with a signature (hard copy or electronic, not verbal) also recording the date and time of approval.

No data may be obtained under the authorisation prior to the date and time recorded against the authorised officer’s signature.

Data obtained prior to the date and time the authorisation was made (notified) is unauthorised and must be quarantined immediately and the relevant quarantine register updated.

Should data be obtained prior to notification to the carrier, and be used by a WA Police Force member, it must be immediately quarantined and legal advice sought.

A case officer should seek an Authorising Officer from within his/her district or division in the first instance however, in circumstances requiring immediate or urgent processing the SOCC provides 24/7 365 access to Authorising Officers to review and authorise an application.

SOCC authorised officers are provided with the ability (in urgent circumstances) to obtain a verbal briefing from a case officer. When considering a verbal briefing the Authorising Officer is required to make sufficient notes in order to demonstrate consideration and satisfaction of the required criteria prior to authorisation (the final authorisation must be in writing).

The location-based services application form provides an area for an Authorising Officer to record notes given during a verbal briefing. The location-based services handbook provides direction and guidance in respect to location-based service applications and authorisation.

No “ping” of a service number/device can occur prior to the Authorising Officer’s endorsement (including date and time, not verbal) of a location-based service application (pages 2 & 3). The investigating officer must inform SOAU by email of the required ping rate at the commencement of the Location-based Service and any subsequent changes to the ping rate.

The Authorising Officer and case officer should record the considerations given to the setting and adjusting of the ping rate, which may be subject to variation throughout the operation.

All documents and notes associated with a LBS application must be retained by the area provisioning the application (SOCC or SOAU) for ombudsman inspection.

sections.h:
An evidentiary certificate may be admitted in evidence as proof of its contents.

Certain individuals who work for a carrier may issue an evidentiary certificate. A carrier’s evidentiary certificate may set out the facts concerning the acts or things done in giving effect to a data authorisation (s 185A(1)).

If a case officer requires an evidentiary certificate, the case officer will submit a request via the IDM RFI request portal. An evidentiary certificate request should include:

Carriage Service Provider.

Date certificate required by.

Original Law Enforcement Agency request reference.

Original Carriage Service Provider reference.

Original Request Type e.g CCR, subscriber check.

Service identifier (number).

An evidentiary certificate or court statement must not be submitted under a Chapter 4 Authority (178, 178A, 179 & 180). A case officer may seek advice from SOAU if assistance is required in obtaining and court statement of evidentiary certificate.

sections.h:
The importance of entering correct information into WA Police systems and registers cannot be understated, errors and incorrect information result in inaccurate ministerial reporting on behalf of the WA Police.

Regardless of the area within WA Police responsible for the provisioning and authorisation of telecommunications data requests, staff MUST make every effort to ensure:

Information entered into all systems and registers is accurate and correctly identifies the relevant data authorisation.

The relevant reference numbers and/or request type is entered correctly into all systems where required including SEDNode, OSTRAVA, RMS and IDM.

The relevant reference numbers and/or request type is entered correctly into all WA Police registers including Quarantine and Revocation registers.

Although a number of systems have been introduced to reduce instances of manual data entry errors, there is still a requirement at certain stages of telecommunications data authorisation and facilitation process which require manual information/data entry.

Incorrect or inaccurate information entry will result in incorrect reporting and subsequently result in adverse findings from the oversight body (Commonwealth Ombudsman). When a critical error has been identified, it will be reported to the oversight body as soon as practicable for review during auditing (self-reporting).

Where possible/applicable areas and staff should apply the appropriate measures to QA manual data entry to reduce/eliminate errors.

sections.h:
The WA Police must report annually to the Minister on a number of matters associated with data authorisations (section 186). This report must be made as soon as practicable after 30 June each year, and within 3 months of 30 June at the latest. In particular, the WA Police must report on:

The number of authorisations made;

The offences in respect of which authorisations were made;

The age of the data sought under an authorisation;

The number of authorisations for subscriber information;

The number of authorisations for traffic data; and

The number of authorisations that were made under journalist information warrants.

The Special Operations Application Unit will prepare these reports on behalf of the Commissioner of Police.

The Special Operations Application Unit will prepare these reports on behalf of the Commissioner of Police.

Data authorisations;

Records which indicate whether authorisations were properly made, including considerations in relation to the privacy requirements set out in section 180F;

Records showing that WA Police notified the relevant carrier of an authorisation Revocations;

Records indicating whether revocations were properly made;

Records showing that WA Police notified the relevant carrier of a revocation;

Documents or materials indicating that any use or disclosure of data authorisation information or accessed data was lawful;

Evidentiary certificates issued by WA Police authorised officers; and Reports given to the Minister under section 186.

sections.t: Threshold for the issue of a journalist information warrant:
sections.h:
Journalist information warrants are issued under section 180T of the TIA Act. An issuing authority may only issue a journalist information warrant if satisfied that the warrant is reasonably necessary for the enforcement of the criminal law, and the public interest in issuing the warrant outweighs the public interest in protecting the confidentiality of the identity of the relevant journalist source, having regard to:

Privacy implications;

The gravity of the matter;

The extent to which the data would be likely to assist in the matter;

Whether reasonable attempts have been made to obtain the information by other means;

Any submissions made by a public interest advocate; and Any other matters that the issuing authority considers relevant.

A warrant must be in place before an authorisation can be made if an authorised officer knows, or reasonably believes, that the subject of the data authorisation would be:

A person who is working in a professional capacity as a journalist, or an employer of such a person; and The purpose of making the authorisation would be to identify the journalist’s source.

Once a journalist information warrant is issued, an authorised officer may make a data authorisation under the authority of the warrant according to normal procedures, as set out above.

Once a journalist information warrant is issued, an authorised officer may make a data authorisation under the authority of the warrant according to normal procedures, as set out above.

sections.h:

If a data authorisation is made under the authority of a journalist information warrant, a copy of the authorisation must be provided to the Commonwealth Ombudsman as soon as practicable after the warrant has expired.

sections.h:
A journalist information warrant enters into force when it is issued (section 180V). It will remain in force for the period specified in the warrant, up to a maximum period of 90 days (section 180U).

The WA Police Commissioner may revoke a journalist information warrant at any time by means of a signed revocation instrument (section 180W(1)(a)). The WA Police Commissioner must revoke a journalist information warrant if he or she is satisfied that the grounds on which the warrant was issued have ceased to exist (section 180W(1)(b)).

sections.h:
Under section 186B of the Act, the Commonwealth Ombudsman is entitled to inspect records which are required to be kept by WA Police under section 185 & 186 of the Act.

Inspections by the Commonwealth Ombudsman

The Commonwealth Ombudsman is required under Chapter 4A (sections 186B-186J) to inspect WA Police Force records to determine the extent of compliance with the data provisions of the TIA Act. The Ombudsman has extensive powers to enter premises and examine records and reports to the Minister about his or her inspections.

Consequently, personnel from the Commonwealth Ombudsman office will attend WA Police Facilities periodically to inspect the records. The Ombudsman’s staff should have unrestricted access to WA Police Force records related to Telecommunications Information. WA Police Force staff must provide the Ombudsman’s inspectors with all necessary assistance to enable them to perform their duties.

The Officer in Charge, SOAU will facilitate any visit by the Commonwealth Ombudsman for the purpose of record inspection, including providing access to Ombudsman inspection staff to WA Police ICT systems to enable the inspection to be conducted.

Case officers who have requested access to data and authorised officers will make themselves available to inspectors at any time requested during an inspection.

The Commonwealth Ombudsman will report to the Minister about the results of its inspections.

sections.t: Authorisations MUST NOT to be made:
sections.h:
An authorisation for sections 178, 178A, 179 and 180 of the Act must not be made under the following circumstances:

An Authorising Officer is NOT satisfied that the request has met the requirements of sections 178, 178A, 179 or 180 of the Act.

The request is for the CONTENT of a communication.

This would require a warrant.

The request is for the information of a journalist, journalist source or an employer of a journalist.

This would require a warrant under section 180J.

The request is for the purposes of a Coroner’s investigation.

This requires a warrant from the Coroner’s Court under section 33(3) of the Coroners Act 1996.

For the purposes of authorisations for Sections 178, 178A and 179 – if the request is for a FUTURE date (Prospective Data).

This will need to be done through section 180.

If the request is for telecommunications business records, not telecommunications products.

This would require an order to produce.

The request is for Telstra 000 recordings / audio. (Note: call register is acceptable).

This must be under section 286 of the Telecommunications Act 1997.

The request is for a Crime Stoppers call.

This is protected under legislation and if in doubt liaison should be made with Crime Stoppers.

The request is for a person /entity KNOWN to the authoriser (conflict of interest) This must be referred to either the Inspector or Superintendent for decision of signature as well as whether to refer to Internal Affairs if the request concerns a Police Officer or Staff member.

The request is for an Evidentiary or Court Certificate.

This does not require Chapter 4 Authorisation.

This would require a warrant under section 180J. The request is for the purposes of a Coroner’s investigation. This requires a warrant from the Coroner’s Court under section 33(3) of the Coroners Act 1996. For the purposes of authorisations for Sections 178, 178A and 179 – if the request is for a FUTURE date (Prospective Data). This will need to be done through section 180. If the request is for telecommunications business records, not telecommunications products. This would require an order to produce. The request is for Telstra 000 recordings / audio. (Note: call register is acceptable). This must be under section 286 of the Telecommunications Act 1997. The request is for a Crime Stoppers call. This is protected under legislation and if in doubt liaison should be made with Crime Stoppers. The request is for a person /entity KNOWN to the authoriser (conflict of interest) This must be referred to either the Inspector or Superintendent for decision of signature as well as whether to refer to Internal Affairs if the request concerns a Police Officer or Staff member. The request is for an Evidentiary or Court Certificate. This does not require Chapter 4 Authorisation.
