sections.t: 1.1 Types of Remote Access Interception Platforms
sections.h: JSI Telecom is the provider of the Telecommunication Interception (TI) platform to the WA Police Force. It provides separate applications for investigators and analysts to access lawfully intercepted TI information (LII) obtained under warrant (TI service warrants and named person warrants) and metadata (data) obtained under TI Authorisation (prospective data and location-based service).
sections.b: JSI Telecom is the provider of the Telecommunication Interception (TI) platform to the WA Police Force. It provides separate applications for investigators and analysts to access lawfully intercepted TI information (LII) obtained under warrant (TI service warrants and named person warrants) and metadata (data) obtained under TI Authorisation (prospective data and location-based service).

sections.h:
UCM is the primary interception platform for the WA Police Force and can be accessed by remote desktop connection via a computer that has been approved for access by SOAU. Access to UCM is limited by the number of licences available to the WA Police Force and in general each remote access facility will only have one or two UCM enabled computers.

UCM is utilised by Officers to live monitor, live map, review intercepted content and metadata, update the workflow of a session or for any data entry that is required (such as document summaries, updating associate details in the Name Number Index as examples).

UCM is also required for investigators to create evidence sets for use and disclosure purposes and for resetting JSI passwords for all applications.

Application for UCM access is to be made via the local District Office and forwarded to SOAU for consideration and approval by Inspector, Crime Support and Services, State Crime.

Approval will be subject to availability of UCM system licenses and a link speed test of the connection into the premises to ensure there is no degradation of other WA Police Force network services.

UCM users can access Speech-To-Text transcription and multilingual translation of intercepted content.

sections.h:
4SE is a desktop application that is available for download via the software centre. Access to the application is managed within UCM and requires users to have a current JSI account and to maintain currency of the Telecommunication Interception Compliance course located on the eForce learning platform.

The WA Police Force implemented 4SE in 2020 to complement the existing UCM platform. With its user friendly and visually driven interface, 4SE promotes in-depth analysis from all intercepted sessions and data types. The filter panel and perspective views expand on the filtering options that can be generated in 4Sight UCM.

4SE users can access Speech-To-Text transcription and multilingual translation of intercepted content.

sections.h:
4Sight Locator is a mobile application that allows real-time or historical viewing of target location information using an WA Police Force mobile device. When your device is registered and enabled on 4Sight UCM, 4Sight Locator uses an encrypted data connection between the mobile device and 4Sight UCM so that you can access your target data.

You can display live or historic location information for one or multiple lines for different targets at the same time. The target’s location information is updated as new data is collected on the 4Sight UCM system. Users need to be mindful of the security of the device when accessing the 4SL platform away from the secure office environment.

When using WA Police Force mobile devices all users need to be aware of and comply with the following:

AD-71.00 Information Systems and Security Policy;

AD-71.04 Remote Access to Police Information Systems Policy;

AD-71.09 Acceptable Computer Use Policy;

CO-01.24 Mobile Devices; and

WA Police Force Code of Conduct and local business area SOPs.

sections.t: 1.2 Procedures for Approval of Remote Access to Lawful Interceptions:
sections.h:
Remote access is defined as any access to telecommunications interception platform from an area external to SOAU.

The detective business unit must establish a secure monitoring environment, manage identified risks and comply with the legislation governing intercepted communication.

These services will only be provided to approved business units when the standards below have been met:

The monitoring environment meets the standards set in this document [refer herein section titled Specific Security Requirements].

The approved members have completed the Telecommunication Interception Compliance course located on the eForce learning platform, have a current JSI account and have been given lawful access to operations containing TI warrants or authorisations.

The detective business unit must establish a secure monitoring environment, manage identified risks and comply with the legislation governing intercepted communication. These services will only be provided to approved business units when the standards below have been met: The monitoring environment meets the standards set in this document [refer herein section titled Specific Security Requirements]. The approved members have completed the Telecommunication Interception Compliance course located on the eForce learning platform, have a current JSI account and have been given lawful access to operations containing TI warrants or authorisations.

sections.h:
The following specific security requirements will be assessed in any consideration of a facility for remote interception access.

The computer must only be used in a secure office environment with only persons permitted to access the LII or data, present.

The computer monitor must be fitted with headphones and must be used when monitoring audio.

Persons not registered at SOAU for the Operation being intercepted or data authorisation may not monitor, or be present during access, unless it has been lawfully disseminated in accordance with the act.

All information obtained, used and disseminated from the interception must be recorded on the Restricted Record Register.

sections.h:
The Commander, State Crime or their delegate (Compliance Unit), is empowered by the Commissioner to ensure compliance to legislation and this policy.

The purpose of the Ethical Standards Division, Compliance Unit, is to provide an independent auditing and assurance function to business units mandated to applying covert investigative methodologies located within the Crime Support and Services Division (CSS). The unit will ensure that State Crime Command are compliant in regards to the State and Commonwealth legislation compliance requirements.

The Commander, State Crime, or their delegate is required to enter upon police premises operating remote monitoring facilities and:

inspect the physical security of the listening room / office environment;

inspect the storage of Records;

inspect any other relevant records held; and

ensure compliance with the legislation by the business unit.

The inspections may be unannounced, and the Compliance Unit are to be afforded immediate and unhindered access to the telecommunications interception monitoring facility and to the security storage area or areas where restricted records are held.

The Management Audit Unit will conduct critique inspections in accordance with Good Governance Guide requirements. The results of these audits will be provided to the Compliance Unit, for follow up and to the Risk Assurance Committee, as per the agencies audit policy.

sections.t: 1.5 Security Requirements and Retention of Records:
sections.h:
[Section 8 (1) of the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Western Australia Act 1996 (WA)] “Investigators are required to store their records in a secure place where it is not accessible to persons other than persons who are entitled to deal with it.”

The case officer must retain the below records:

Original written or electronic records of lawfully intercepted information;

Each use made by the agency of lawfully intercepted information;

Each disclosure of lawfully intercepted information by an officer of the agency; and Each occasion when lawfully intercepted information was produced in a judicial proceeding.

sections.h:
SOAU have decentralised live telephone interception. This has given investigators timely access to interceptions, allowing them to coordinate dynamic operations in “real time”. The procedures have been designed to increase officer’s awareness to the interception legislation and procedural requirements.

Adherence to these procedures will reduce the incidence of intercepted evidence being excluded from judicial proceedings and reduce non-compliance risks. Should you have any queries please contact the SOAU Manager on 08 9223 3511.

sections.h:
The SOAU business unit previously held responsibility within this agency for the interception of live communications. Increasing demand for SOAU services has dictated a shift to remote access monitoring by approved registered officers within their own business units (JSI access granted).

These guidelines are designed to detail the legislative and procedural obligations, which apply to members engaged in remote access monitoring and reviewing.

Remote access monitoring is the process where SOAU provides the technical capability to monitor an intercepted communication and the case officer is responsible for monitoring, reviewing and managing the product.

These procedures are intended to complement and reinforce the relevant responsibilities and record keeping functions carried out by the WA Police Force when executing warrants under the Telecommunications (Interception & Access) Act 1979 (Cth) (the Commonwealth Act) and complying with the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Western Australia Act 1996 (WA) (the State Act).

sections.t: The Commonwealth Act has two main objectives:
sections.h: To protect the privacy of individuals using telecommunications in this country, by making it an offence to intercept communications passing over the telecommunications networks [section 7(1) of the Commonwealth Act]; and To allow the lawful interception of communications in certain circumstances by law enforcement and intelligence agencies.
sections.b: To protect the privacy of individuals using telecommunications in this country, by making it an offence to intercept communications passing over the telecommunications networks [section 7(1) of the Commonwealth Act]; and To allow the lawful interception of communications in certain circumstances by law enforcement and intelligence agencies.

sections.t: Section 46 of the Commonwealth Act provides:
sections.h:
Where an agency applies to an eligible Judge or nominated AAT Member for a warrant in respect of a telecommunications service and the Judge or nominated AAT member is satisfied … that information that would be likely to be obtained by intercepting under a warrant, communications made to or from the service would be likely to assist in connection with the investigation by the agency of a serious offence.

Serious Offence in general terms is an offence that is punishable by imprisonment for life or for a minimum period of 7 years and meets a specific criteria as defined by the Act [a serious offence is explicitly defined in section 5D of the Commonwealth Act].

Lawfully Intercepted Information (LII) can only be used for a Permitted Purpose [the definition of a permitted purpose is contained within section 5 of the Commonwealth Act] and states that, in general terms, LII can be used for the purposes of an investigation by the agency or eligible authority of a Prescribed Offence. A Prescribed Offence in general terms is the investigation of an offence that is punishable by imprisonment for a minimum of 3 years.
sections.b: Where an agency applies to an eligible Judge or nominated AAT Member for a warrant in respect of a telecommunications service and the Judge or nominated AAT member is satisfied … that information that would be likely to be obtained by intercepting under a warrant, communications made to or from the service would be likely to assist in connection with the investigation by the agency of a serious offence. Serious Offence in general terms is an offence that is punishable by imprisonment for life or for a minimum period of 7 years and meets a specific criteria as defined by the Act [a serious offence is explicitly defined in section 5D of the Commonwealth Act]. Lawfully Intercepted Information (LII) can only be used for a Permitted Purpose [the definition of a permitted purpose is contained within section 5 of the Commonwealth Act] and states that, in general terms, LII can be used for the purposes of an investigation by the agency or eligible authority of a Prescribed Offence. A Prescribed Offence in general terms is the investigation of an offence that is punishable by imprisonment for a minimum of 3 years.

sections.t: Using Interception Information to Investigate Another Offence:
sections.h:
In the event that an investigator discovers another Prescribed Offence during the course of an interception it may be used in the subsequent investigation. It cannot be used for offences that do not meet the Prescribed Offence definition as detailed in the legislation.

The investigator should use caution when using interception information for this purpose.

Further disclosure can only be made for a Permitted Purpose.

An officer investigating the original offence can use interception information to investigate a Prescribed Offence.

An officer investigating the original offence can disclose interception information to another investigator not involved in the original investigation, to allow the investigation of the Prescribed Offence.

An officer investigating the original offence who wishes to disclose to another agency, must do so by liaising with SOAU in compliance with the provisions of section 68 of the Commonwealth Act.

sections.h:
An investigator is empowered to communicate information in the following circumstances:

Section 67(1) of the Commonwealth Act allows:

(1) An officer or staff member of an agency may, for a permitted purpose, or permitted purposes, in relation to the agency, and for no other purpose, communicate to another person, make use of, or make a record of the following:

(a) lawfully intercepted information other than foreign intelligence information;

(b) interception warrant information.

This communication is to assist the investigator to conduct the investigation and includes enlisting an interpreter, translator and other police officers and must be within the constraints of the legislation.

This communication is to assist the investigator to conduct the investigation and includes enlisting an interpreter, translator and other police officers and must be within the constraints of the legislation.

sections.t: Communicating Restricted Information to Another Interception Agency:
sections.h:
Section 68 of the Commonwealth Act, empowers the Commissioner or a person authorised by the Commissioner to communicate lawfully intercepted information to another interception agency in certain specific circumstances.

No officer, unless subject to a section 68 authorisation from the Commissioner, may communicate information to an external agency.

Contact the Officer in Charge of SOAU for any interagency communication.
sections.b: Section 68 of the Commonwealth Act, empowers the Commissioner or a person authorised by the Commissioner to communicate lawfully intercepted information to another interception agency in certain specific circumstances. No officer, unless subject to a section 68 authorisation from the Commissioner, may communicate information to an external agency. Contact the Officer in Charge of SOAU for any interagency communication.

sections.t: Telecommunications Interception Information is NOT intelligence:
sections.h:
Section 63 of the Commonwealth Act, prevents the communication of intercepted product in contravention of Section 7. If you are investigating a secondary offence of Indecent Assault, with a penalty of 2 years imprisonment, this offence falls short of the threshold for prescribed offences which is 3 years imprisonment. The intercepted information cannot be used to progress an investigation into the secondary offence, no further communication is allowed to any other person.

Lawful Interception information is not to be used for populating intelligence databases, not to be placed on Statement of material facts, Briefing notes or Incident reports. Not to be copied into WAPOL systems such as Silva, Teams, WebEOC, Outlook. Interception information may only be used for a permitted purpose - using interception information for intelligence purposes is a breach of the Commonwealth Act.

Intercepted information can only be accessed by the investigation team (including supervisors of the original investigation) and then only disclosed further for a permitted purpose.

The intercepted information cannot be used to progress an investigation into the secondary offence, no further communication is allowed to any other person. Lawful Interception information is not to be used for populating intelligence databases, not to be placed on Statement of material facts, Briefing notes or Incident reports. Not to be copied into WAPOL systems such as Silva, Teams, WebEOC, Outlook. Interception information may only be used for a permitted purpose - using interception information for intelligence purposes is a breach of the Commonwealth Act. Intercepted information can only be accessed by the investigation team (including supervisors of the original investigation) and then only disclosed further for a permitted purpose.

sections.t: Section 105 of the Commonwealth Act provides a penalty for such breaches:
sections.h:
(1) A person who contravenes subsection 7(1) or section 63 is guilty of an offence against that subsection or section.

(2) An offence against subsection 7(1) or section 63 is an indictable offence and, subject to this section, is punishable on conviction by imprisonment for a period not exceeding 2 years.
sections.b: (1) A person who contravenes subsection 7(1) or section 63 is guilty of an offence against that subsection or section. (2) An offence against subsection 7(1) or section 63 is an indictable offence and, subject to this section, is punishable on conviction by imprisonment for a period not exceeding 2 years.

sections.t: Using Interception Information to Investigate Another Offence:
sections.h:
In the event that an investigator discovers another prescribed offence during the course of an interception, it may be used in the subsequent investigation. It cannot be used for offences that do not meet the prescribed offences definition, pursuant to Section 5 of the Commonwealth Act.

The investigator should use caution in using interception information for this purpose.

Further disclosure can only be made for a permitted purpose.

In summary:

An officer investigating the original offence can use interception information to investigate a prescribed offence.

An officer investigating the original offence can disclose interception information to another investigator not involved in the original investigation, to allow the investigation of the prescribed offence.

An officer investigating the original offence who wishes to disclose to another agency, must do so by liaising with SOAU OIC or Manager in line with the provisions of section 68 of the Commonwealth Act [refer herein, section titled, Communicating restricted information to another interception agency].

sections.h:
The investigating officer needs to ensure the following:

At the commencement of an operation the investigator is responsible for completing the Investigator Notification Sheet for SOAU monitoring staff which includes:

Officer contact details / on call officers, etc.

Objectives of the operation / profile of the target, POI’s.

Operational phrases / key words / associates / meetings of interest, etc.

Explanation of what SOAU can do for investigator.

The IO needs to be in regular contact with SOAU to supply operational updates [if unable to attend in person an email to Station Mail will suffice] Arrange regular review of screened call sessions, supply names of officers responsible for reviewing the calls so they can be added to the operation.

Prompt interpretation of foreign language calls if applicable See Speech-To-Text and Translation in Section 4.

As a security measure, if contacted by a staff member of SOAU to relay information during the operation:

SOAU Member says: “Message from the lobby”; and

Investigator replies with their: “Name, rank & regimental number”

If they don’t get that response, the SOAU staff member will hang up immediately.

Storage of all TI product / associated paperwork, etc. is to be in a safe/lockable cabinet.

Investigator is to immediately disclose to SOAU any issues of corruption or misconduct by notifying the Interception Supervisors on 9223 3550.

Prompt notification to SOAU via e-mail must be done should a warrant no longer be required so that it can be revoked.

Completion of an effectiveness report for each warrant must be done no later than seven days after the warrant’s termination/revocation.

sections.h: Currently SOAU is maintaining 4 models of interception:
sections.b: Currently SOAU is maintaining 4 models of interception:

sections.h:
In this model SOAU maintains responsibility for managing the intercepted product and will compile a brief summary of each call intercepted. Investigators will have access to all intercepted product and it is the responsibility of the investigator to review each session and select the appropriate classification and review status [refer section titled Review Status and Classifications hereunder].

The investigator can also access the SOAU Monitored model, but must advise the supervisor on duty at SOAU of their intention. The SOAU monitors will not review or complete summaries of the intercepted product relevant to the specified operation whilst the investigator is accessing the product, as it will disrupt the continuity of the intercepted sessions. It is the investigators responsibility to act as a monitor, reviewing the product, compiling a summary and selecting the appropriate classification and review status [refer section titled Review Status and Classifications hereunder]. On completion the investigator must advise the SOAU supervisor that they have ceased monitoring to enable the SOAU monitors to resume interception.

SOAU will notify the investigator by phone of any information of note or specifically requested by the investigator. If the information intercepted is deemed to be of a critical nature by SOAU the investigator will receive a phone call and email advising them of the information intercepted.

If the investigator uses discretion to mitigate an offence, it is their responsibility to submit a discretionary report.
sections.b: In this model SOAU maintains responsibility for managing the intercepted product and will compile a brief summary of each call intercepted. Investigators will have access to all intercepted product and it is the responsibility of the investigator to review each session and select the appropriate classification and review status [refer section titled Review Status and Classifications hereunder]. It is the investigators responsibility to act as a monitor, reviewing the product, compiling a summary and selecting the appropriate classification and review status [refer section titled Review Status and Classifications hereunder]. On completion the investigator must advise the SOAU supervisor that they have ceased monitoring to enable the SOAU monitors to resume interception. SOAU will notify the investigator by phone of any information of note or specifically requested by the investigator. If the information intercepted is deemed to be of a critical nature by SOAU the investigator will receive a phone call and email advising them of the information intercepted. If the investigator uses discretion to mitigate an offence, it is their responsibility to submit a discretionary report.

sections.h:
In this model SOAU maintains responsibility for managing the intercepted product and will compile a brief summary of each call intercepted, however SOAU will withhold from the investigator intercepted product that they are precluded from by a condition of the warrant or by the request of the investigator.

An example of this would be where a condition has been placed on the warrant by a Judge precluding the investigator from having access to certain product such as legal privilege calls. Therefore, in this case all calls deemed by SOAU to be legal privilege would be withheld from the investigator. It remains the responsibility of the investigator to review all other sessions and select the appropriate classification and review status [refer section titled Review Status and Classifications hereunder].

SOAU will notify the investigator by phone of any information of note or specifically requested by the investigator. If the information intercepted is deemed to be of a critical nature by SOAU the investigator will receive a phone call and email advising them of the information intercepted.

If the investigator uses discretion to mitigate an offence, it is their responsibility to submit a discretionary report.

If the information intercepted is deemed to be of a critical nature by SOAU the investigator will receive a phone call and email advising them of the information intercepted. If the investigator uses discretion to mitigate an offence, it is their responsibility to submit a discretionary report.

sections.h: In this model SOAU facilitates the technical capability to intercept telecommunications and it is the responsibility of the investigator to monitor and manage the intercepted product. It is also a requirement that the investigator compiles a brief summary on JSI of each call intercepted and selects the appropriate classification and review status [refer herein section titled Review Status and Classifications].

sections.h:
SOAU have implemented Speech-To-Text transcription and translation technology through JSI. This transcription is available to view in both UCM and Explorer programmes on 4sight.

The Transcription and Translation documents, can be located on the ‘documents’ tab of the session within the session details.

Language will automatically be set to English, unless otherwise detected through the JSI translation programme, the languages detected will be populated onto the result. The results also show a percentage as to the confidence on the transcriptions accuracy.

SOAU staff have the capability to re-run the session in another language and should be contacted if this is required.

The investigating officer is to assess the product produced and determine if it requires professional translation.

PLEASE NOTE: Any AI-generated translation or transcription is considered an intelligence product only. It must not be used as evidence, disclosed or copied outside the JSI system, or acted upon operationally until the content has been verified by directly examining the original source material (e.g., audio or text from the intercepted session).

sections.t: 5 Procedures for Dealing with Calls and Session Classifications:

sections.h:
If an investigator uncovers any session of “Corruption/Misconduct” nature including allegations of the investigators attached to the relevant case.

The investigator must immediately advise SOAU Interception Supervisor by telephone and as directed, e-mail the details of the Operation Name, Warrant Number, Session Number, and a brief synopsis of the allegation/s contained within the session directly to that Interception Supervisor.

The SOAU Interception Supervisor will review and advise Internal Affairs if necessary.

The investigator must immediately advise SOAU Interception Supervisor by telephone and as directed, e-mail the details of the Operation Name, Warrant Number, Session Number, and a brief synopsis of the allegation/s contained within the session directly to that Interception Supervisor. The SOAU Interception Supervisor will review and advise Internal Affairs if necessary.

sections.h:
The JSI status of the session must be designated “Legal Privilege”.

Intercepted calls between the person of interest and their legal representative for the purpose of communicating with their client or gain instructions to act for that client for legal matters, is deemed to be legal professional privilege.

A legal representative includes a solicitor, barrister, a member of their staff or an agent acting on their behalf.

This information must not be disseminated or acted upon, it is a common law immunity to preserve the confidentiality of legal communications.

Legal professional privilege cannot be attached to communications for the purpose of facilitating a crime or there is a higher public interest.

It is recommended that direction be sought from WA Police Force, Legal Services before acting upon any communication that has the potential to be defined as legal professional privilege.

sections.h:
If the intercepted communication identifies a risk of injury to persons, damage to property or the commission of an offence. The investigator is to immediately notify their supervisor to decide what action is to be taken.

If the case officer uses discretion to mitigate an offence, it is their responsibility to submit a discretionary report.

sections.h:
When a foreign language call is intercepted, the following steps must be followed to ensure appropriate review and operational use:

Review the AI Translation

Investigators should first review the AI-generated translation available in the JSI system. This translation is considered an intelligence product only and must not be used as evidence or acted upon operationally without verification.

Determine Need for Professional Interpretation

If the content of the call is required for evidentiary purposes or operational action, a professional interpreter must be engaged through the agency’s approved translation service process.

Engage Translation Services

Submit a request for interpretation via the existing agency process. If assistance is required, contact SOAU staff for support in initiating or managing this process.

Upload Summary to JSI Interpretation Panel

Once the professional interpretation is complete, a summary of the call should be placed in the JSI system’s interpretation panel.

Mark as ‘Interpretation Complete’

The interpreted call must be classified as ‘Interpretation Complete’ in the system. This ensures SOAU staff are notified and can proceed with reviewing the interpreted content.

Interpreters are subject to confidentiality agreements to access LII, so please liaise with SOAU if this is required.

WARNING – The accuracy of the JSI translation cannot be verified without a professional translation service.

sections.h:
Not Classified – system default for new calls; SOAU monitors to review.

Investigator Review – for investigator to review.

Foreign – for investigator to arrange interpretation.

Review - Relevant – session believed to be relevant to operation.

Review - Not Relevant – session not believed to be relevant to operation.
sections.b: Not Classified – system default for new calls; SOAU monitors to review. Investigator Review – for investigator to review. Foreign – for investigator to arrange interpretation. Review - Relevant – session believed to be relevant to operation. Review - Not Relevant – session not believed to be relevant to operation.

sections.h:
The Review Status should update automatically as the session Classification is changed.

Designators used are:

Not Viewed – system default for new calls; SOAU monitors to review.

Investigator Review Required – for investigator to review.

Completed – call has been reviewed by an investigator.

Interpretation Required – used to identify sessions requiring interpretation, once interpretation completed investigator must select appropriate review status – arranging an interpretation is the responsibility of the investigator.
